title: B-02.5: Update frontend Login.tsx for two-step MFA flow
description:
SUBTASK of B-02. Depends on B-02.2 and B-02.3 (backend must be ready first).

Modify web/src/pages/Login.tsx to handle the new two-step MFA login response:

CURRENT FRONTEND FLOW:
1. User enters username → calls /login/prepare → gets MFA status
2. If TOTP: shows TOTP input, calls /login/totp with {username, code}
3. If passkey: calls /login/passkey/options with ?username=X, then /login/passkey/verify
4. Stores returned JWT in localStorage

NEW FRONTEND FLOW:
1. User enters username → calls /login/prepare → gets MFA status (no change)
2. User enters password → calls /login with {username, password}
3. If response has mfa_required=true:
   a. Store mfa_token from response in component state (NOT localStorage)
   b. Show MFA input (TOTP code or passkey button)
   c. If TOTP: call /login/totp with {mfa_token, code} instead of {username, code}
   d. If passkey: call /login/passkey/options with ?mfa_token=X, then /login/passkey/verify
4. Store the final full JWT (from step 3c/d) in localStorage (or cookie per F-01)

KEY UX CONSIDERATION: The mfa_token has a 5-minute TTL. If the user takes too long entering their TOTP code, the pending token expires and they need to re-enter their password. Consider showing a countdown or at least a clear error message when the pending token expires.

FILE: web/src/pages/Login.tsx

title: F-09: Disable source maps in production builds
description:
SEVERITY: MEDIUM (SECURITY_REVIEW.md F-09) | OWASP A05 — Security Misconfiguration

PROBLEM: web/vite.config.ts has minimal configuration (lines 1-8) with no explicit sourcemap setting. Default Vite behavior may generate and serve source maps in production, giving attackers access to the full original TypeScript source code, making reverse engineering trivial.

FIX: Add explicit sourcemap: false to vite.config.ts build section:
  build: {
    sourcemap: false
  }

One-line change. Verify after by checking that no .map files appear in web/dist/ after 'npm run build'.

FILES:
- web/vite.config.ts

SCOPE: S (~15 min) | BREAKING: No | RISK: None

title: B-02.3: Modify front-end MFA handlers to require pending MFA token
description:
SUBTASK of B-02. Depends on B-02.1 and B-02.2.

Modify internal/http/api/front/handlers/mfa.go:

LoginTOTP (lines 542-576):
- CURRENT: Accepts {username, code} → validates TOTP → issues full JWT
- NEW: Accepts {mfa_token, code} → validates pending MFA token → extracts userID → loads user → validates TOTP → issues full JWT
- Remove username from loginTotpRequest struct, add mfa_token field

LoginPasskeyOptions:
- CURRENT: Accepts username query param → returns passkey challenge
- NEW: Accepts mfa_token query param → validates → extracts username → returns challenge

LoginPasskeyVerify (lines 630-710):
- CURRENT: Accepts username query param → validates passkey → issues full JWT
- NEW: Accepts mfa_token query param → validates → extracts user → validates passkey → issues full JWT

KEY CONSIDERATION: The passkey session store (passkeyLoginSessions) currently keys on username. After this change, it should key on the pending MFA token's JTI or userID to prevent session confusion.

FILE: internal/http/api/front/handlers/mfa.go
close_reason: Implemented in current working tree; front MFA handlers now require pending MFA token and targeted handler tests pass

title: B-02.2: Modify front-end login handler to return pending MFA token
description:
SUBTASK of B-02. Depends on B-02.1 (PendingMFAClaims must exist).

Modify internal/http/api/front/handlers/auth.go Login() handler:

CURRENT FLOW: Validate password → if valid, issue full JWT immediately (MFA status ignored during login).

NEW FLOW:
1. Validate username + password (existing logic, no change)
2. After successful password validation, check if user has MFA enabled:
   - Check user.TOTPSecret != '' (TOTP enabled)
   - Check len(user.PasskeyID) > 0 (passkey enabled)
3. If MFA enabled: return HTTP 200 with {mfa_required: true, mfa_token: '<pending JWT>', mfa_methods: ['totp', 'passkey']}
4. If MFA NOT enabled: return full JWT as today (zero change for non-MFA users)

BACKWARD COMPATIBILITY: Non-MFA users see zero change. MFA users get a new response shape — the frontend (Login.tsx) must handle this. API consumers calling /login directly will need to handle the mfa_required response.

FILE: internal/http/api/front/handlers/auth.go
close_reason: Implemented in current working tree; user Login returns pending MFA token and targeted handler tests pass

title: F-10: Align frontend password validation with backend complexity rules
description:
SEVERITY: LOW (SECURITY_REVIEW.md F-10) | OWASP A07

PROBLEM: Client-side validation at web/src/pages/Register.tsx enforces only a 6-character minimum. Once B-10 implements 8-char minimum with letter+digit requirements on the backend, the frontend validation will be inconsistent — users will pass frontend validation but fail on backend submission, creating a confusing UX.

FIX: Update frontend form validation to match B-10 backend rules:
- Minimum 8 characters
- At least one letter
- At least one digit
- Show real-time validation feedback as the user types

DEPENDS ON: B-10 (backend rules must be finalized first so frontend matches)

FILES:
- web/src/pages/Register.tsx (registration form validation)
- web/src/pages/Login.tsx (if password change is handled here)
- Any other password input components

SCOPE: S (~2 hours) | BREAKING: No

title: F-06: Add noopener,noreferrer to window.open calls
description:
SEVERITY: MEDIUM (SECURITY_REVIEW.md F-06) | OWASP A05 — Security Misconfiguration

PROBLEM: window.open() calls in the frontend do not consistently use noopener,noreferrer flags. The opened page can access the opener via window.opener and redirect it to a phishing page (reverse tabnapping attack).

FOUND LOCATIONS:
- web/src/pages/admin/AuthFiles.tsx:1445 — window.open(modalUrl, '_blank') — MISSING flags
- web/src/components/admin/VersionUpdateButton.tsx:15 — window.open(data.releaseUrl, '_blank', 'noopener,noreferrer') — CORRECT (already fixed)

FIX: Add 'noopener,noreferrer' third argument to all window.open calls that don't have it. Search for all occurrences with: grep -rn 'window.open' web/src/ and fix any missing the flags.

SCOPE: S (~30 min) | BREAKING: No | RISK: None

title: B-11: Remove full API key from list response, keep only masked prefix
description:
SEVERITY: MEDIUM (SECURITY_REVIEW.md B-11) | OWASP A01 — Broken Access Control

PROBLEM: The serializeAPIKey function at internal/http/api/front/handlers/api_keys.go:98-117 returns BOTH the full key ('key': row.APIKey) AND a masked version ('key_prefix': first8...last4). API keys should only be revealed once at creation time. Returning them repeatedly in list responses exposes them to XSS attacks, browser extensions, network logging, and any future vulnerability that can read API responses.

CURRENT CODE:
  return gin.H{
    'key':        row.APIKey,        // FULL KEY — should not be here
    'key_prefix': prefix,            // Masked version — this is correct
    ...
  }

FIX:
1. Remove 'key': row.APIKey from serializeAPIKey (list/get responses)
2. Keep 'key_prefix' as the masked display version
3. Return full key ONLY in Create and Regenerate responses (the one-time reveal)
4. Add a note/warning in the Create response: 'This is the only time the full key will be shown'

CONSIDERATION: Check if the admin handler at internal/http/api/admin/handlers/api_keys.go has the same issue (the exploration found the admin list doesn't return the key field, but verify).

FILES:
- internal/http/api/front/handlers/api_keys.go:98-117 (remove 'key' from serializeAPIKey)

SCOPE: S (~2 hours) | BREAKING: Minor (clients reading 'key' from list responses will get undefined)

title: B-12: Bound io.ReadAll with LimitReader to prevent memory exhaustion DoS
description:
SEVERITY: MEDIUM (SECURITY_REVIEW.md B-12) | OWASP A05 — Security Misconfiguration

PROBLEM: io.ReadAll(c.Request.Body) is called without size limits in three locations. An attacker can send a multi-gigabyte request body to exhaust server memory and crash the process.

LOCATIONS:
- internal/http/api/admin/handlers/mfa.go:667 (passkey login verify — reads WebAuthn assertion)
- internal/http/api/front/handlers/mfa.go:665 (same, user-facing)
- internal/http/api/admin/handlers/auth_files.go:206 (auth file upload)

FIX: Wrap all three with io.LimitReader:
  const maxBodySize = 1 << 20 // 1 MB
  rawBody, err := io.ReadAll(io.LimitReader(c.Request.Body, maxBodySize))

WHY 1MB: WebAuthn assertions are typically < 5KB. Auth files are configuration YAML, typically < 100KB. 1MB provides generous headroom while preventing abuse. If larger payloads are legitimately needed, the limit can be made configurable per-endpoint.

ALSO CONSIDER: Gin has c.Request.Body = http.MaxBytesReader(w, r.Body, maxSize) as an alternative that returns a proper 413 status. However, LimitReader is simpler for these specific read-all patterns and the calling code already handles errors.

SCOPE: S (~2 hours) | BREAKING: No (legitimate payloads are well under 1MB) | RISK: None

title: B-09: Change PostgreSQL SSL default from 'disable' to 'require'
description:
SEVERITY: MEDIUM (SECURITY_REVIEW.md B-09) | OWASP A02 — Cryptographic Failures

PROBLEM: BuildDSN at internal/app/init.go:60-72 defaults sslmode to 'disable' when not explicitly set. The docker-compose connection string also uses sslmode=disable. Database credentials and all query data transit in plaintext — vulnerable to network sniffing and MITM attacks.

FIX: Change default from 'disable' to 'require':
  if sslMode == '' { sslMode = 'require' }

BREAKING CHANGE: Existing installations using local unencrypted PostgreSQL connections will fail to connect after this change. They must explicitly set sslmode=disable in their config or DATABASE_URL. This is intentional — the secure default protects new deployments, while existing deployments that consciously chose unencrypted can opt back in.

Add documentation comment in docker-compose.yml explaining the sslmode options.

FILES:
- internal/app/init.go:61 (change default sslMode)
- docker-compose.yml (document sslmode in DATABASE_URL)

SCOPE: S (~1 hour) | BREAKING: Potentially | RISK: Low

title: F-12: Implement server-side token revocation on logout
description:
SEVERITY: LOW (SECURITY_REVIEW.md F-12) | OWASP A07 — Identification and Authentication Failures

PROBLEM: Logout at web/src/utils/auth.ts only clears localStorage on the client. The JWT remains valid on the backend until it expires (up to 30 days per B-14, or 24h after B-14 fix). A stolen token remains fully usable after the legitimate user logs out — the user has NO way to invalidate a compromised session.

FIX — REQUIRES B-07 JTI CLAIM:
1. Backend: Create POST /v0/front/logout and POST /v0/admin/logout endpoints
2. When called, extract the JTI (JWT ID) from the token and add it to a denylist
3. Denylist storage options:
   a. Redis SET with TTL matching token expiry (recommended — auto-cleanup, shared across instances)
   b. Database table with periodic cleanup job (works without Redis)
   c. In-memory map with TTL (single-instance only, lost on restart)
4. Modify ParseToken and ParseAdminToken to check JTI against denylist after parsing
5. Frontend: logout handler calls POST /logout before clearing local state

DEPENDS ON: B-07 (JTI claim must exist in tokens — without JTI, there's nothing to denylist)

ARCHITECTURE CONSIDERATION: If using Redis, reuse the existing Redis connection from internal/ratelimit/manager.go. If the deployment doesn't have Redis, fall back to in-memory with a log warning about single-instance limitation.

FILES:
- internal/security/jwt.go (add denylist check to parse functions)
- internal/http/api/front/front.go (register /logout route)
- internal/http/api/admin/admin.go (register /logout route)
- New handlers for logout endpoints
- web/src/utils/auth.ts (call backend logout)

SCOPE: M (~8 hours) | BREAKING: No

comments.author: iznogoud
comments.text:
ARCHITECTURE DECISION — TOKEN DENYLIST STORAGE:

This feature requires checking every authenticated request against a denylist of revoked JTI values. The choice of storage backend has significant performance and operational implications:

Option A (recommended): Redis SET with TTL
  + Auto-cleanup: entries expire with the token's original TTL
  + Shared across instances: works in horizontally scaled deployments
  + Fast: O(1) SISMEMBER check
  + Already available: ratelimit.Manager already uses Redis
  - Requires Redis (already optional dep for rate limiting)
  - Lost on Redis restart (acceptable — tokens just live until natural expiry)

Option B: Database table
  + Persistent across restarts
  + No additional infrastructure
  - Adds DB query to EVERY authenticated request (hot path)
  - Requires periodic cleanup job for expired entries
  - Slower than Redis

Option C: In-memory map with TTL
  + Simplest implementation
  + Fastest (local memory)
  - Not shared across instances (logout on instance A doesn't affect instance B)
  - Lost on restart

RECOMMENDATION: Use Redis if available (check ratelimit config), fall back to in-memory with a log warning. Do NOT use database — the per-request overhead is unacceptable for an API gateway.

IMPLEMENTATION SKETCH:
  // In ParseToken, after successful parse:
  if denylist.Contains(claims.ID) {
    return nil, ErrRevokedToken
  }
  
  // In logout handler:
  ttl := claims.ExpiresAt.Time.Sub(time.Now())
  denylist.Add(claims.ID, ttl)

title: B-08: Remove GORM Debug() call that leaks API keys to logs
description:
SEVERITY: MEDIUM (SECURITY_REVIEW.md B-08) | OWASP A09 — Security Logging and Monitoring Failures

PROBLEM: internal/http/api/front/handlers/api_keys.go:322 uses h.db.Debug() in the production Revoke handler. GORM Debug mode logs complete SQL queries — including raw API key values — to stdout/log files. Any log aggregation system (ELK, Datadog, CloudWatch) or person with log access obtains user API keys in plaintext.

FIX: Remove .Debug() — one line change:
  BEFORE: res := h.db.Debug().WithContext(c.Request.Context()).Model(&models.APIKey{})
  AFTER:  res := h.db.WithContext(c.Request.Context()).Model(&models.APIKey{})

Also search for any other .Debug() calls in production handlers and remove them.

SCOPE: S (~30 min) | BREAKING: No | RISK: None

title: B-02: Implement two-step MFA login to prevent password bypass
description:
SEVERITY: CRITICAL (SECURITY_REVIEW.md B-02) | OWASP A07 — Identification and Authentication Failures

PROBLEM: LoginTOTP endpoints (admin mfa.go:544-578, front mfa.go:542-576) accept only username + 6-digit TOTP code and issue a full JWT — NO password is ever validated. MFA replaces password authentication rather than supplementing it. TOTP has only 1,000,000 possible values; without rate limiting (B-05), this is brute-forceable. Similarly, LoginPasskeyVerify (admin mfa.go:632-712, front mfa.go:630-710) issues JWT based solely on passkey assertion without requiring password.

ATTACK SCENARIO:
1. Attacker knows a valid admin username (e.g., 'admin')
2. Calls POST /v0/admin/login/totp with username and brute-forces all 1M TOTP codes
3. On success, full admin JWT issued without ever knowing the password
4. Complete system compromise achieved

DESIGN — TWO-STEP LOGIN:
Step 1: POST /login validates password. If MFA enabled, returns short-lived (5-min) 'pending MFA' JWT token (not a full session JWT). If no MFA, returns full JWT as today.
Step 2: POST /login/totp or /login/passkey/verify validates the pending MFA token + MFA factor, THEN issues full JWT.

This requires a new PendingMFAClaims type in jwt.go, modifications to login handlers in both admin and front, modifications to all MFA handlers, and frontend Login.tsx updates. See subtasks for detailed breakdown.

REUSE: sessionStore pattern with TTL at mfa.go:42-91 (reference only — pending MFA uses signed JWT for horizontal scaling). security.CheckPassword already exists for password validation.

SCOPE: L (~16 hours) | BREAKING: Yes (intentional) | RISK: Medium (complex change across 6 files)

comments.author: iznogoud
comments.text:
DESIGN RATIONALE — WHY JWT OVER IN-MEMORY STORE FOR PENDING MFA:

Option A (chosen): Signed JWT with 5-min TTL as pending MFA token
  + Stateless — any server instance can validate (horizontal scaling)
  + No shared state needed between instances
  + Natural expiry via JWT exp claim
  + Smaller attack surface (cryptographically signed)
  - Slightly larger payload in API responses
  - Cannot be revoked before expiry (but 5-min window is acceptable)

Option B (rejected): In-memory session store (like existing passkeyLoginSessions)
  + Simpler implementation
  + Can be revoked immediately
  - Single-instance only — breaks horizontal scaling
  - Memory leak risk (see B-13 for existing session store issues)
  - Lost on server restart

Option C (rejected): Database-backed session
  + Persistent, shared across instances
  + Can be revoked
  - Adds DB queries to the hot login path
  - Requires cleanup job
  - Over-engineered for a 5-minute token

COORDINATED DEPLOYMENT: This change MUST be deployed alongside the frontend update (B-02.5). If backend deploys first, MFA users cannot log in (old frontend sends username, new backend expects mfa_token). If frontend deploys first, non-MFA login still works but MFA login breaks when the old backend returns a full JWT instead of mfa_required. Safest: deploy both simultaneously or use feature flag.

title: Phase 1: Critical Security Fixes — Account Takeover & Auth Bypass
description: IMMEDIATE action required. These vulnerabilities allow unauthenticated account takeover (B-01), authentication bypass via MFA (B-02), and indefinite use of expired API keys (B-04). Combined, they represent complete compromise vectors for the LLM gateway. All 3 must be resolved before any other security work proceeds. Estimated: 3-5 days.

comments.author: iznogoud
comments.text:
OVERARCHING GOAL: CLIProxyAPIBusiness is a production multi-tenant LLM API gateway that proxies requests to Anthropic, OpenAI, Gemini, and other providers. It handles billing, access control, model routing, and usage tracking for enterprise customers. The security review (SECURITY_REVIEW.md, 2026-03-15) identified 29 vulnerabilities — 3 Critical, 9 High, 10 Medium, 7 Low.

Phase 1 addresses the three findings that allow UNAUTHENTICATED account takeover:
- B-01: Password reset without any verification (CRITICAL)
- B-02: MFA login that doesn't require password (CRITICAL)  
- B-04: Expired API keys still accepted (HIGH)

These are not theoretical risks. B-01 can be exploited by anyone who knows a username+email. B-02 can be brute-forced (1M TOTP combinations, no rate limiting). Together they represent complete system compromise vectors.

COMPLETION CRITERIA: Phase 1 is complete when:
1. All three fixes are deployed
2. Tests verify the attack scenarios are blocked
3. Manual penetration testing confirms the fixes work
4. go test ./... passes with no regressions

title: B-01: Require authentication for password reset endpoint
description:
SEVERITY: CRITICAL (SECURITY_REVIEW.md B-01) | OWASP A07 — Identification and Authentication Failures

PROBLEM: POST /v0/front/reset-password at internal/http/api/front/handlers/auth.go:150-189 allows ANY unauthenticated caller to set a new password for ANY user account by supplying only username, email, and new_password. No email verification token, no time-limited link, no CAPTCHA, no rate limiting. An attacker who knows or scrapes a username+email can immediately and silently take over any account. This is the single most dangerous vulnerability in the system.

ATTACK SCENARIO:
1. Attacker enumerates valid usernames via login endpoint response differences
2. Attacker obtains email via OSINT or prior data breach
3. Attacker calls POST /v0/front/reset-password with {username, email, new_password}
4. User's password is changed. Attacker logs in with new credentials.

FIX APPROACH: Move /reset-password behind JWT auth middleware. The endpoint already has a parallel authenticated version at authed.PUT('/profile/password', profileHandler.ChangePassword) — so the cleanest approach is to deprecate /reset-password in favor of the existing authenticated endpoint. Short-term: move route into authed group, require current_password, get identity from JWT. Long-term: implement email-based token reset flow (requires email infrastructure).

FILES:
- internal/http/api/front/front.go (move route from public to authed group, line 31 → after line 35)
- internal/http/api/front/handlers/auth.go:150-189 (rewrite handler: remove username/email from request, use JWT userID, require current_password)
- web/src/ (update any frontend reset-password flow)

SCOPE: M (~8 hours) | BREAKING: Yes (intentional) | RISK: Low (fixing a vulnerability)
close_reason: Completed: moved reset-password behind auth, delegated to authenticated password change flow, and added regression tests

comments.author: iznogoud
comments.text:
ARCHITECTURAL DECISION: We chose to move /reset-password behind auth middleware rather than implementing email-based token reset. Rationale:
1. The project has NO email-sending infrastructure (no SMTP config, no email templates, no email service)
2. Building email infrastructure just for password reset is disproportionate effort
3. An authenticated 'change password' endpoint already exists at PUT /profile/password
4. The /reset-password endpoint's only legitimate use case (forgot password while logged out) requires email verification to be secure — without it, the endpoint is inherently dangerous

DEPRECATION STRATEGY: Keep the /reset-password route registered but behind auth middleware. Log a deprecation warning when called. Remove entirely in the next major version. Document the change in CHANGELOG.

FUTURE: If email infrastructure is added (for notifications, billing alerts, etc.), revisit implementing a proper email-based reset flow with time-limited tokens.

title: B-17: Move version endpoint behind admin authentication
description:
SEVERITY: LOW (SECURITY_REVIEW.md B-17) | OWASP A05 — Security Misconfiguration

PROBLEM: GET /v0/version at internal/http/api/admin/admin.go:29 is registered without authentication middleware. It exposes the application version, commit hash, and build date to unauthenticated callers. This aids attackers in identifying the exact version to look up known vulnerabilities or tailor exploits.

FIX OPTIONS:
A) Move the version endpoint behind adminAuthMiddleware (simple, but prevents health checks)
B) Return only {status: 'ok'} to unauthenticated callers; return full version info to authenticated admins
C) Remove the endpoint entirely and expose version only in the admin dashboard

RECOMMENDED: Option B — keeps it useful for health monitoring while hiding version details.

FILES:
- internal/http/api/admin/admin.go:29 (move or modify route registration)

SCOPE: S (~1 hour) | BREAKING: Minor (unauthenticated version consumers lose version details)

title: F-07: Strip console.* statements from production builds
description:
SEVERITY: MEDIUM (SECURITY_REVIEW.md F-07) | OWASP A09 — Security Logging and Monitoring Failures

PROBLEM: 123 console.error and console.log calls across 32 frontend files log internal error details, API response structures, and stack traces to the browser console. This aids attackers with XSS or physical access in understanding the system's internal behavior, API shapes, and error handling patterns.

HIGH-CONCENTRATION FILES:
- web/src/pages/admin/AuthFiles.tsx: 19 occurrences
- web/src/pages/admin/Models.tsx: 11 occurrences
- web/src/pages/admin/BillingRules.tsx: 9 occurrences

FIX: Add esbuild drop option to web/vite.config.ts for production builds:
  esbuild: { drop: ['console'] }

This strips ALL console.* calls at build time — zero manual edits needed across the 32 files. The console methods are removed from the compiled output entirely (not just silenced), so there is no runtime overhead.

ALTERNATIVE: vite-plugin-remove-console npm package, but the built-in esbuild drop is simpler and has no additional dependency.

CONSIDERATION: During development, console statements remain (the drop only applies to production builds). If specific console statements are needed in production for operational monitoring, replace them with a proper logging library that can be configured by log level.

FILES:
- web/vite.config.ts (add esbuild drop config)

SCOPE: S (~30 min) | BREAKING: No | RISK: None

title: B-10: Create shared password complexity validator
description:
SEVERITY: MEDIUM (SECURITY_REVIEW.md B-10) | OWASP A07 — Identification and Authentication Failures

PROBLEM: Password validation is inconsistent and weak across the codebase:
- internal/app/init.go:355 — only enforces 6-char minimum for initial admin setup
- internal/http/api/front/handlers/auth.go:35-92 — no minimum for user registration (just non-empty)
- internal/http/api/admin/handlers/users.go:36-80 — no minimum for admin-created users (just non-empty)

While bcrypt hashing at cost 12 (internal/security/password.go) is properly implemented, weak passwords defeat the purpose of strong hashing. A 1-character password hashed with bcrypt-12 is still crackable.

FIX:
1. Create internal/security/password_validator.go:
   func ValidatePassword(password string) error
   Rules: minimum 8 characters, at least one letter (unicode.IsLetter), at least one digit (unicode.IsDigit)
   Returns descriptive error messages suitable for API responses

2. Call ValidatePassword from all password-setting endpoints:
   - internal/app/init.go:355 (initial admin setup)
   - internal/http/api/front/handlers/auth.go (Register handler)
   - internal/http/api/front/handlers/auth.go (ResetPassword / ChangePassword handler)
   - internal/http/api/admin/handlers/users.go (CreateUser handler)

3. Create internal/security/password_validator_test.go with table-driven tests:
   - Empty string → error
   - 7 chars → error (too short)
   - 8 chars all letters → error (no digit)
   - 8 chars all digits → error (no letter)
   - 8 chars mixed → pass
   - Unicode letters → pass

SCOPE: S (~4 hours) | BREAKING: No (strengthens validation) | RISK: None

comments.author: iznogoud
comments.text:
PASSWORD VALIDATION RULES — RATIONALE:

Why 8 characters minimum (not 6, not 12, not 16):
- NIST SP 800-63B recommends minimum 8 characters for memorized secrets
- 6 characters (current) is too short — even with bcrypt-12, offline attacks are feasible
- 12+ characters would be more secure but creates friction for a B2B product where admins often set initial passwords for users
- 8 is the industry standard balance point

Why letter + digit (not uppercase + lowercase + special):
- Complex character class requirements lead to predictable patterns (Password1!, Welcome123!)
- NIST explicitly recommends AGAINST complex composition rules
- Letter + digit prevents purely numeric PINs and purely alphabetic dictionary words
- Users can still use passphrases ('correct horse battery staple 42') which are stronger than complex short passwords

IMPLEMENTATION NOTE: Use unicode.IsLetter and unicode.IsDigit to support international characters. Don't restrict to ASCII — a Japanese user should be able to use kanji in their password.

title: B-02.6: Add tests for two-step MFA login flow
description:
SUBTASK of B-02. Depends on B-02.1 through B-02.4.

Create comprehensive tests verifying the two-step MFA login flow works correctly and the old bypass is blocked:

TESTS TO ADD:

1. internal/security/jwt_test.go (or extend existing):
   - TestGeneratePendingMFAToken_ValidToken: generates token, parses successfully, has correct Purpose
   - TestParsePendingMFAToken_ExpiredToken: token with past expiry returns ErrExpiredToken
   - TestParsePendingMFAToken_WrongPurpose: regular user/admin token rejected by ParsePendingMFAToken
   - TestParsePendingMFAToken_InvalidSignature: tampered token rejected

2. Handler tests (following existing pattern in db_api_key_provider_test.go):
   - TestLoginTOTP_WithoutPendingToken_Returns401: calling /login/totp with {username, code} but no mfa_token returns 401
   - TestLoginTOTP_WithExpiredPendingToken_Returns401: expired pending token rejected
   - TestLoginTOTP_WithValidPendingToken_And_ValidCode_ReturnsJWT: full happy path
   - TestLoginTOTP_WithValidPendingToken_And_InvalidCode_Returns401: wrong TOTP code rejected
   - TestLoginPasskey_WithoutPendingToken_Returns401: bare username passkey login blocked

TESTING PATTERN: Use in-memory SQLite via glebarez/sqlite, httptest.NewRequest, gin.CreateTestContext (established project pattern from selector_user_group_test.go).

FILES: internal/security/jwt_test.go, new test files for handlers
close_reason: Added pending MFA JWT invalid-signature coverage plus front/admin TOTP and passkey regression tests; targeted and full project verification passed

title: B-07: Add Issuer and JTI claims to JWT tokens for namespace separation
description:
SEVERITY: HIGH (SECURITY_REVIEW.md B-07) | OWASP A01 — Broken Access Control

PROBLEM: Both GenerateToken (user) and GenerateAdminToken (admin) at internal/security/jwt.go use the same JWT_SECRET and jwt.SigningMethodHS256 with NO Issuer, Audience, Subject, or ID claim. UserClaims has UserID, AdminClaims has AdminID — but ParseToken and ParseAdminToken do not validate that the correct ID field is present or that an issuer matches. A user token CAN be parsed by ParseAdminToken (it will have AdminID=0 and Username set) — the middleware then looks up admin ID 0, which fails, but this is defense by accident, not by design.

FIX:
1. GenerateToken: Add Issuer='cpab:user', ID=uuid.New().String() to RegisteredClaims
2. GenerateAdminToken: Add Issuer='cpab:admin', ID=uuid.New().String()
3. ParseToken: After parsing, validate claims.Issuer=='cpab:user'. Reject otherwise.
4. ParseAdminToken: After parsing, validate claims.Issuer=='cpab:admin'. Reject otherwise.
5. Add github.com/google/uuid dependency if not present

JTI (ID) RATIONALE: The JTI claim enables future token revocation (F-12). By adding it now, we avoid a second breaking token change later. The JTI is stored in the token but not checked against a denylist yet — that comes in Phase 4.

BREAKING CHANGE: All existing tokens (user and admin) will be rejected because they lack the Issuer claim. Every user and admin must re-login. Deploy during low-traffic window and communicate ahead of time. The default 30-day token expiry means there is no natural rollover — it's a hard cutover.

FILES:
- internal/security/jwt.go (add Issuer, ID to both generate functions; validate Issuer in both parse functions)
- go.mod (add uuid dependency if needed)
- internal/security/jwt_test.go (test cross-type rejection)

SCOPE: M (~8 hours) | BREAKING: Yes (forces re-login) | RISK: Medium

comments.author: iznogoud
comments.text:
BREAKING CHANGE ROLLOUT STRATEGY:

This change invalidates ALL existing JWTs (user and admin) because old tokens lack the Issuer claim that ParseToken/ParseAdminToken will now require. Every user and admin must re-login.

ROLLOUT OPTIONS:
1. Hard cutover (recommended for small deployments): Deploy during low-traffic window. All sessions end. Users re-login within minutes.
2. Graceful migration (for large deployments): Deploy with a temporary flag that accepts tokens WITHOUT Issuer claim for 24h, logging a warning. After 24h, flip the flag to strict mode. This gives users time to naturally re-login.
3. Version header (most complex): Issue new tokens with Issuer on login, accept both old and new for a transition period based on token version.

RECOMMENDATION: Option 1 for now. The default 30-day expiry (B-14) means there's no natural rollover. A hard cutover with advance notice (email/announcement) is the cleanest approach. After B-14 reduces expiry to 24h, future breaking token changes will naturally roll over within a day.

JTI CLAIM FORWARD-COMPATIBILITY: We add the JTI (uuid) claim now even though we don't check it against a denylist yet. This avoids a SECOND breaking token change when F-12 (server-side logout) is implemented. The JTI is just carried in the token payload at negligible cost.

title: B-13: Cap MFA in-memory session stores with size limits and periodic cleanup
description:
SEVERITY: MEDIUM (SECURITY_REVIEW.md B-13) | OWASP A04 — Insecure Design

PROBLEM: passkeyLoginSessions, passkeyRegistrationSessions, and totpPendingSecrets at internal/http/api/admin/handlers/mfa.go:142-149 (and front mfa.go:140-148) are unbounded in-memory maps. Expired entries are only cleaned up lazily on access (checked when Get is called). An attacker can trigger thousands of passkey login sessions with different usernames, consuming memory without bound. Since cleanup only happens on Get, if nobody accesses those specific keys, they persist forever.

FIX:
1. Add maxSize field to sessionStore and secretStore structs (recommend 10,000 entries)
2. In Set(): if len(items) >= maxSize, evict oldest entry (by creation time) before adding new one
3. Start a background cleanup goroutine when the store is created:
   - Run every 5 minutes
   - Delete all entries where createdAt + TTL < now
   - Use sync.Once or init pattern to ensure only one goroutine per store
4. Apply to both admin and front mfa.go files (parallel structure)

CONSIDERATION: After B-02 is implemented, the MFA session stores become less critical (pending MFA state moves to JWT tokens). However, the passkey registration stores will still be used for the registration ceremony, so cleanup is still needed.

FILES:
- internal/http/api/admin/handlers/mfa.go:142-149 (add maxSize, cleanup goroutine)
- internal/http/api/front/handlers/mfa.go:140-148 (same)

SCOPE: M (~4 hours) | BREAKING: No | RISK: Low

title: F-01: Migrate JWT storage from localStorage to httpOnly cookies
description:
SEVERITY: CRITICAL (SECURITY_REVIEW.md F-01) | OWASP A02/A03 — Cryptographic Failures / Injection

PROBLEM: Both admin and user JWT tokens are stored in localStorage (web/src/api/config.ts:8-11, Login.tsx:189). Any JavaScript executing in the page context — XSS, malicious browser extension, compromised npm dependency — can read localStorage and steal tokens, gaining full authenticated access as the victim. localStorage is accessible to ALL JavaScript on the same origin with no access control.

FIX — BACKEND:
1. On successful login, set JWT in response cookie: Set-Cookie: token=<jwt>; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Strict; Path=/
2. On logout, clear the cookie: Set-Cookie: token=; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Strict; Path=/; Max-Age=0
3. Modify auth middleware (admin JWT middleware in admin.go, user JWT middleware in front.go) to read token from:
   a. Cookie first (new behavior)
   b. Authorization: Bearer header as fallback (backward compat for API consumers)
4. CORS must support credentials: Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true (requires B-03 non-wildcard CORS)

FIX — FRONTEND:
1. web/src/api/config.ts — remove localStorage.getItem(TOKEN_KEY_*) from request interceptors; browser sends cookie automatically
2. web/src/pages/Login.tsx — remove localStorage.setItem(TOKEN_KEY_FRONT, data.token); the cookie is set by the backend
3. web/src/utils/auth.ts — logout calls POST /logout endpoint instead of just clearing localStorage

DEPENDS ON: B-03 (CORS must not be wildcard for credentials to work), B-07 (token namespacing should be in place)

SCOPE: L (~8 hours) | BREAKING: No (Authorization header still accepted as fallback)

comments.author: iznogoud
comments.text:
COORDINATED CHANGE — THIS IS THE MOST COMPLEX TASK IN PHASE 2.

This task touches both backend (cookie setting) and frontend (stop reading localStorage). The implementation must be backward-compatible: API consumers using Authorization: Bearer header must continue to work. Only the web UI frontend switches to cookies.

BACKEND COOKIE IMPLEMENTATION:
  http.SetCookie(c.Writer, &http.Cookie{
    Name:     "cpab_token",
    Value:    jwt,
    Path:     "/",
    HttpOnly: true,
    Secure:   true,       // requires HTTPS
    SameSite: http.SameSiteStrictMode,
    MaxAge:   int(expiry.Seconds()),
  })

AUTH MIDDLEWARE READING ORDER:
  1. Check Cookie header for cpab_token (new path)
  2. If no cookie, check Authorization: Bearer header (backward compat)
  3. If neither, return 401

TESTING CHECKLIST:
  [ ] Web UI login sets httpOnly cookie (verify via DevTools → Application → Cookies)
  [ ] Web UI requests automatically include cookie (no Authorization header needed)
  [ ] API consumers using Authorization: Bearer still work
  [ ] Logout clears cookie (Set-Cookie with MaxAge=0)
  [ ] Cookie not accessible from JavaScript (document.cookie returns empty for cpab_token)
  [ ] Cross-origin requests blocked without CORS credentials (B-03 dependency)

title: B-05: Add rate limiting to all authentication endpoints
description:
SEVERITY: HIGH (SECURITY_REVIEW.md B-05) | OWASP A07 — Identification and Authentication Failures

PROBLEM: All authentication endpoints (/login, /login/totp, /login/passkey/*, /register, /reset-password) are registered outside any rate-limiting middleware. The existing Selector rate limiter only applies to LLM proxy paths (/v1, /v1beta, /api). This enables unbounded brute-force attacks against passwords, TOTP codes (only 1M combinations for B-02), and the password reset endpoint.

EXISTING INFRASTRUCTURE: A fully functional rate limiting system already exists at internal/ratelimit/manager.go with:
- Manager struct supporting both memory and Redis backends
- Circuit breaker for Redis failover to in-memory
- Allow(ctx, key, limit) API that returns Result{Allowed bool}
- Already used for LLM proxy rate limiting

FIX: Create a Gin middleware wrapper around the existing ratelimit.Manager and apply it to all unauthenticated auth routes in both admin.go and front.go.

IMPLEMENTATION:
1. Create authRateLimitMiddleware(rlManager *ratelimit.Manager) gin.HandlerFunc
   - Key: 'auth:' + c.ClientIP()
   - Limit: 5 requests per minute (configurable)
   - On rejection: HTTP 429 with Retry-After header and JSON error body
2. Apply to auth route groups in front.go:25-31 and admin.go:39-43
3. Pass ratelimit.Manager through RegisterFrontRoutes/RegisterAdminRoutes signatures
4. Wire the manager instance in app.go route setup

CONSIDERATION: Per-IP limiting can be circumvented by distributed attacks. Per-username limiting is also valuable but must handle non-existent usernames carefully to avoid DoS on legitimate users. Start with per-IP, add per-username in a follow-up.

FILES:
- internal/http/api/front/front.go (add middleware, apply to auth routes)
- internal/http/api/admin/admin.go (same)
- internal/app/app.go (pass manager instance to route registration)
- internal/ratelimit/ (reuse existing Manager — no changes needed)

SCOPE: M (~4 hours) | BREAKING: No | RISK: Low

comments.author: iznogoud

title: F-13: Remove or replace stale /reset-password login link
description: FOLLOW-UP from cliproxyapibusiness-fk5. The login page at web/src/pages/Login.tsx links to /reset-password, but web/src/App.tsx does not define that route. After B-01, public unauthenticated password reset is intentionally disabled, so this link is now both broken and conceptually stale. Fix by either removing the link or replacing it with copy that points authenticated users to /settings or the in-app password change flow. Files: web/src/pages/Login.tsx, web/src/App.tsx, any relevant copy/translations. Scope: S (~30-60 min).

title: B-02.1: Add PendingMFAClaims type and generate/parse functions to jwt.go
description:
SUBTASK of B-02. First step in the two-step MFA login implementation.

Add to internal/security/jwt.go:

1. New PendingMFAClaims struct:
   - UserID uint64, Username string, Purpose string ('pending_mfa')
   - jwt.RegisteredClaims with 5-minute TTL
   - Purpose field prevents token confusion (a pending token cannot be used as a session token)

2. GeneratePendingMFAToken(secret string, userID uint64, username string) (string, error):
   - Creates JWT with Purpose='pending_mfa', Issuer='cpab:pending_mfa', 5-min expiry
   - Uses same signing method (HS256) and secret as other tokens

3. ParsePendingMFAToken(secret string, tokenString string) (*PendingMFAClaims, error):
   - Validates HMAC signature, expiry, and Purpose=='pending_mfa'
   - Rejects tokens with wrong Purpose (prevents reuse of session tokens as MFA tokens)

DESIGN RATIONALE: Using a signed JWT (rather than in-memory session store) for the pending MFA state enables horizontal scaling — any server instance can validate the token without shared state. The 5-minute TTL limits the brute-force window.

FILE: internal/security/jwt.go

comments.author: iznogoud
comments.text:
CODE PLACEMENT: Add the new types and functions AFTER the existing AdminClaims/GenerateAdminToken/ParseAdminToken block (after line 106 of jwt.go). Keep the file organized: UserClaims section → AdminClaims section → PendingMFAClaims section.

PENDING TOKEN PURPOSE FIELD: The Purpose field ('pending_mfa') serves as a discriminator that prevents token confusion attacks. Without it, a regular user session JWT could be passed as an mfa_token to bypass MFA. The ParsePendingMFAToken function MUST reject tokens where Purpose != 'pending_mfa'.

ISSUER COORDINATION WITH B-07: This task adds PendingMFAClaims with its own Issuer ('cpab:pending_mfa'). B-07 later adds Issuer to UserClaims ('cpab:user') and AdminClaims ('cpab:admin'). These don't conflict — they're different claim types with different Issuers. But implementers should be aware that jwt.go is modified by both tasks.

title: F-08: Validate releaseUrl against domain allowlist before navigation
description:
SEVERITY: MEDIUM (SECURITY_REVIEW.md F-08) | OWASP A01 — Broken Access Control

PROBLEM: If the server response includes a releaseUrl field used in window.open() or redirect, a compromised backend or MITM attacker can redirect admin users to arbitrary external URLs (phishing, malware download).

LOCATION: web/src/components/admin/VersionUpdateButton.tsx — consumes releaseUrl from API response and opens it.

FIX:
1. Create a URL validation utility that checks the URL against an allowed domain list:
   const ALLOWED_DOMAINS = ['github.com', 'gitlab.com']
   function isAllowedUrl(url: string): boolean — parse URL, check hostname against allowlist
2. In VersionUpdateButton.tsx, validate releaseUrl before calling window.open
3. If URL fails validation, show a warning to the user with the actual URL and ask for confirmation

SCOPE: S (~1 hour) | BREAKING: No | RISK: None

title: B-03: Replace wildcard CORS with configurable origin allowlist
description:
SEVERITY: HIGH (SECURITY_REVIEW.md B-03) | OWASP A05 — Security Misconfiguration

PROBLEM: The CORS middleware at internal/app/cors.go:12 unconditionally sets Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * with Authorization in Access-Control-Allow-Headers. This permits any origin to make cross-origin requests carrying JWT tokens to all admin and user management endpoints. A victim visiting an attacker-controlled page could have their authenticated API calls silently replayed.

CURRENT CODE (cors.go:10-24):
  c.Header('Access-Control-Allow-Origin', '*')
  c.Header('Access-Control-Allow-Methods', 'GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, OPTIONS')
  c.Header('Access-Control-Allow-Headers', 'Origin, Content-Type, Accept, Authorization, X-API-Key, X-Goog-Api-Key')

FIX:
1. Make allowed origins configurable via config.yaml field 'cors_origins' and env var CORS_ORIGINS (comma-separated)
2. Modify corsMiddleware(allowedOrigins []string) to:
   - Check request Origin header against the allowed list
   - If matched, reflect that specific origin (not *)
   - If no match, omit the Access-Control-Allow-Origin header (browser blocks request)
   - If list contains '*', allow all (development mode only)
   - Add 'Vary: Origin' header when reflecting specific origins (required by spec)
3. When F-01 (cookie migration) is implemented, also add Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true

IMPORTANT CONTEXT: LLM proxy endpoints (/v1/*, /v1beta/*) are called by CLI tools and SDKs, NOT browsers — CORS restrictions do not affect them. Only the admin/front management endpoints need CORS configuration. This means the change primarily affects the web UI.

FILES:
- internal/app/cors.go (parameterize origins, add Vary header)
- internal/config/config.go (add CORSOrigins []string field)
- docker-compose.yml (add CORS_ORIGINS env var documentation)

SCOPE: M (~4 hours) | BREAKING: Potentially (if frontend served from different origin) | RISK: Medium

comments.author: iznogoud
comments.text:
CORS NUANCE: The wildcard '*' origin has a subtle interaction with credentials. Per the CORS spec, Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * is INCOMPATIBLE with Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true. When F-01 migrates to httpOnly cookies, the CORS policy MUST reflect specific origins (not *) and include Allow-Credentials: true.

MIGRATION PATH:
1. This task (B-03): Make origins configurable, default to same-origin only
2. F-01 (later): Add Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true to cors.go

CONFIG DESIGN: Use a comma-separated env var CORS_ORIGINS for simplicity:
  CORS_ORIGINS=https://admin.example.com,https://portal.example.com
  
If empty or unset, default to same-origin only (no ACAO header emitted). If set to '*', allow all origins (dev mode with a log warning).

LLM PROXY ENDPOINTS: The /v1/* and /v1beta/* endpoints are consumed by CLI tools (Claude Code, Codex, etc.) that don't run in browsers and don't send Origin headers. CORS configuration does NOT affect these endpoints. Only the admin/front management web UI is affected.

title: Phase 2: High-Severity Auth Hardening — Rate Limiting, CORS, JWT, Cookies
description: This-week priority. Addresses infrastructure-level weaknesses that amplify Phase 1 risks: no brute-force protection (B-05), wildcard CORS allowing cross-origin attacks (B-03), weak/default JWT secrets (B-06), JWT namespace confusion enabling privilege escalation (B-07), and frontend auth stored in XSS-vulnerable localStorage (F-01, F-02, F-03, F-05). These don't allow direct account takeover alone but significantly lower the attack barrier. Estimated: 5-7 days.

comments.author: iznogoud
comments.text:
PHASE 2 STRATEGIC CONTEXT: Phase 1 plugs the direct account-takeover holes. Phase 2 hardens the infrastructure so that even if new vulnerabilities are discovered, the blast radius is limited:

- Rate limiting (B-05) prevents brute-force attacks from succeeding even if new auth flaws emerge
- CORS (B-03) prevents cross-origin exploitation of any endpoint  
- JWT secret hardening (B-06) prevents token forgery
- JWT namespacing (B-07) prevents privilege escalation between user and admin contexts
- Cookie migration (F-01) prevents XSS from stealing session tokens
- Frontend auth hardening (F-02, F-03, F-05) reduces information leakage and social engineering vectors

DEPLOYMENT ORDER within Phase 2: B-05, B-03, B-06 are independent and can be deployed in any order. B-07 must come after B-02 (Phase 1) since both touch jwt.go. F-01 must come after both B-03 and B-07. F-02, F-03, F-05 are independent frontend changes.

title: F-11: Display HTTP warning on initialization page
description:
SEVERITY: LOW (SECURITY_REVIEW.md F-11) | OWASP A02 — Cryptographic Failures

PROBLEM: During the initialization wizard at web/src/pages/Init.tsx, admin credentials (database DSN containing password, admin password, JWT secret) are submitted to the backend. If TLS is not enforced at the deployment level, these critical secrets transit in plaintext over the network.

FIX:
1. In the Init.tsx component, check window.location.protocol on mount
2. If protocol is 'http:' (not 'https:'), display a prominent warning banner:
   'WARNING: You are submitting sensitive credentials over an unencrypted connection. Use HTTPS in production to protect your database password and admin credentials.'
3. Allow the user to proceed (they may be on localhost for development) but make the risk clear

CONSIDERATION: This is a defense-in-depth measure. TLS termination is ultimately the deployment operator's responsibility (typically via reverse proxy like nginx/Caddy). But a visible warning prevents accidental insecure setup.

FILES:
- web/src/pages/Init.tsx

SCOPE: S (~1 hour) | BREAKING: No

title: Phase 3: Medium-Severity Hardening — Input Validation, Logging, Headers
description: Next-sprint priority. Addresses defense-in-depth gaps: debug SQL logging leaking API keys (B-08), unencrypted DB connections (B-09), weak passwords (B-10), API key exposure in list responses (B-11), DoS via unbounded reads (B-12, B-13), missing security headers (B-15), and frontend hygiene (F-06 through F-09). These are exploitable but require more specific conditions or insider access. Estimated: 3-4 days.

title: F-04: Add CSRF protection for cookie-based authentication
description:
SEVERITY: HIGH (SECURITY_REVIEW.md F-04, deferred to Phase 4) | OWASP A01 — Broken Access Control

PROBLEM: Currently irrelevant because JWT is in Authorization header (not cookies). However, once F-01 (cookie migration) is implemented, CSRF becomes an active threat — any website can trigger state-changing requests that automatically include the session cookie.

FIX: Implement the double-submit cookie CSRF pattern:
1. On login, backend generates a random CSRF token and sets it in TWO places:
   - A cookie: csrf_token=<random>; SameSite=Strict; Secure (readable by JavaScript — NOT httpOnly)
   - Included in the login response body for initial setup
2. Frontend reads the csrf_token cookie and includes it as X-CSRF-Token header on all state-changing requests (POST, PUT, DELETE)
3. Backend middleware validates that X-CSRF-Token header matches the csrf_token cookie value
4. SameSite=Strict on the session cookie provides additional protection

DEPENDS ON: F-01 (cookie migration must be complete first — no point adding CSRF protection while tokens are in localStorage)

WHY DOUBLE-SUBMIT: It's stateless (no server-side CSRF token storage needed), works with the existing JWT architecture, and is recommended by OWASP for SPA applications.

FILES:
- Backend: new CSRF middleware, login handler modifications
- Frontend: axios interceptor to read csrf cookie and add header

SCOPE: M (~6 hours) | BREAKING: No

comments.author: iznogoud
comments.text:
WHY DOUBLE-SUBMIT COOKIE (not Synchronizer Token or Origin header check):

1. Synchronizer Token Pattern: Requires server-side state (store CSRF tokens per session). For a stateless JWT-based API gateway, this adds complexity and state management. Rejected.

2. Origin/Referer Header Check: Simple but can be bypassed in some edge cases (browser bugs, proxy stripping). Not robust enough for a security-focused fix. Rejected as sole defense.

3. Double-Submit Cookie (chosen): The server generates a random CSRF token and sets it as a non-httpOnly cookie. The frontend reads this cookie via JavaScript and sends it as an X-CSRF-Token header. The server validates that the header matches the cookie. This works because:
   - A cross-origin attacker CAN cause the browser to send the cookie (via CSRF)
   - But they CANNOT read the cookie to put it in the header (same-origin policy)
   - So the header/cookie mismatch blocks the attack
   
   Combined with SameSite=Strict on the session cookie, this provides defense-in-depth.

TIMING: This task only matters AFTER F-01 (cookie migration). While JWTs are in localStorage and sent via Authorization header, CSRF is not a concern (JavaScript must explicitly add the header, which cross-origin scripts cannot do).

title: B-14: Reduce default JWT expiry from 30 days to 24 hours
description:
SEVERITY: LOW (SECURITY_REVIEW.md B-14) | OWASP A07 — Identification and Authentication Failures

PROBLEM: defaultJWTExpiry at internal/config/config.go:86 is set to 30 * 24 * time.Hour (720 hours). A stolen JWT remains valid for a full month. No token revocation mechanism exists beyond disabling the admin/user record in the database — and even that requires an admin to notice the compromise.

FIX: Change defaultJWTExpiry to 24 * time.Hour. This balances security (shorter compromise window) with usability (users log in once per day rather than once per month).

FUTURE: Implement refresh tokens for long-lived sessions. The refresh token has a longer lifetime but can only be used to obtain new access tokens, not to make API calls directly. This is a larger architectural change and belongs in a separate epic.

CONSIDERATION: Existing deployments that haven't set a custom JWT_EXPIRY will see their effective expiry drop from 30 days to 24 hours. Users will need to re-login daily. Document this in the changelog. Operators can set JWT_EXPIRY=720h to restore the old behavior if they accept the risk.

FILES:
- internal/config/config.go:86 (change defaultJWTExpiry)

SCOPE: S (~30 min) | BREAKING: Behavioral change (shorter sessions)

title: Phase 4: Low-Severity Polish — Expiry, Logout, CSRF, Misc Hardening
description: Backlog priority. Addresses remaining findings that improve security posture but have limited direct exploitability: excessive JWT lifetime (B-14), admin password change without verification (B-16), unauthenticated version endpoint (B-17), CSRF protection for cookie-based auth (F-04), frontend password validation alignment (F-10), HTTP warning on init page (F-11), and server-side token revocation on logout (F-12). Several depend on Phase 2 work. Estimated: 2-3 days.

title: F-02: Add ProtectedRoute component with backend token validation
description:
SEVERITY: HIGH (SECURITY_REVIEW.md F-02) | OWASP A01 — Broken Access Control

PROBLEM: All protected routes in web/src/App.tsx:37-73 (admin dashboard, user management, billing, etc.) rely solely on client-side checks. AdminEntryRedirect at lines 32-35 checks localStorage token presence but does not validate it with the backend. A user can navigate directly to /admin/dashboard with a valid (or even expired/forged) token and the page renders — data protection relies entirely on backend API responses returning 401, but the route itself renders with loading states and partial UI.

FIX:
1. Create web/src/components/ProtectedRoute.tsx:
   - On mount, calls GET /v0/front/profile (user routes) or GET /v0/admin/me (admin routes) to validate the token
   - If valid: render children
   - If 401/403: redirect to login page, clear any stale tokens
   - Show loading spinner during validation (not the protected content)
2. Update web/src/App.tsx:37-73 — wrap all admin/user routes with <ProtectedRoute> 
3. The validation call should be cached for the session (e.g., React context) to avoid hitting the backend on every route navigation, but must re-validate on page refresh.

CONSIDERATION: This is a frontend-only change. The backend already enforces auth on API calls — this just prevents the UI from rendering misleadingly before the first API call fails.

FILES:
- web/src/components/ProtectedRoute.tsx (new)
- web/src/App.tsx (wrap routes)

SCOPE: M (~4 hours) | BREAKING: No

title: B-02.4: Mirror MFA login changes for admin handlers
description:
SUBTASK of B-02. Depends on B-02.1.

Apply the same two-step MFA login pattern to the admin endpoints. The admin and front handler files are structurally parallel (nearly identical code), so the changes mirror B-02.2 and B-02.3.

FILES:
- internal/http/api/admin/handlers/auth.go — modify Login() to return pending MFA token when admin has MFA enabled (same pattern as front auth.go)
- internal/http/api/admin/handlers/mfa.go — modify LoginTOTP (lines 544-578) and LoginPasskeyVerify (lines 632-712) to require pending MFA token instead of bare username

NOTE: Admin MFA uses respondWithAdminToken() helper (which calls security.GenerateAdminToken). The pending MFA token should use the same GeneratePendingMFAToken regardless of admin/user — the distinction happens when the full token is issued after MFA validation.
close_reason: Implemented in current working tree; admin login and MFA handlers mirror pending MFA flow and targeted handler tests pass

title: B-06: Eliminate insecure default JWT secret and add startup validation
description:
SEVERITY: HIGH (SECURITY_REVIEW.md B-06) | OWASP A02 — Cryptographic Failures

PROBLEM: Multiple weak/default secrets exist:
1. internal/app/init.go:189-194 — generateJWTSecret() falls back to hardcoded 'change-me-to-a-secure-random-string' if crypto/rand fails
2. docker-compose.yml:29 — JWT_SECRET defaults to 'insecure-jwt-secret-change-me'
3. docker-compose.yml:8 — POSTGRES_PASSWORD defaults to 'insecure-change-me'

If any deployment uses these defaults, an attacker can forge arbitrary admin and user JWT tokens, granting full system control. The fallback in init.go is especially dangerous because it silently degrades to an insecure state.

FIX:
1. init.go generateJWTSecret(): Return error instead of fallback. If crypto/rand fails, the system MUST NOT start with a known-weak secret. Propagate error to caller.
2. config.go LoadJWTConfig or equivalent: After all resolution (env var → config file → default), check if final secret is empty or matches known-weak list. If so, log.Fatal with instructions: 'JWT_SECRET must be set to a strong random value (min 32 chars). Generate with: openssl rand -hex 32'
3. docker-compose.yml: Remove all default values for secrets. Add comments explaining what each env var needs.

CONSIDERATION: This will break existing deployments that rely on default secrets. This is INTENTIONAL — those deployments are currently compromised. The startup error message must be clear and actionable so operators can fix it in < 1 minute.

FILES:
- internal/app/init.go:189-194 (remove fallback, return error)
- internal/config/config.go (add weak-secret validation at startup)
- docker-compose.yml (remove defaults, add documentation comments)

SCOPE: S (~3 hours) | BREAKING: Yes (intentional) | RISK: Low

comments.author: iznogoud
comments.text:
STARTUP VALIDATION PATTERN: The weak-secret check should run very early in the application startup (in main.go or config loading), BEFORE any HTTP server starts listening. This prevents a window where the application accepts requests with a forged JWT.

KNOWN-WEAK SECRET LIST:
  knownWeak := []string{
    "",
    "change-me-to-a-secure-random-string",    // init.go fallback
    "insecure-jwt-secret-change-me",           // docker-compose default
    "secret",                                   // common test value
    "jwt-secret",                               // common placeholder
  }

Also check length: if len(secret) < 32, log a WARNING (not fatal) suggesting a longer secret. The 32-char minimum is a recommendation, not a hard requirement, to avoid breaking edge cases.

ERROR MESSAGE must be actionable:
  log.Fatal("FATAL: JWT_SECRET is weak or unset. Set a strong random value (min 32 chars).\n" +
    "Generate one with: openssl rand -hex 32\n" +
    "Set via environment: JWT_SECRET=<value> or in config.yaml: jwt.secret: <value>")

title: B-15: Add security response headers to all HTTP responses
description:
SEVERITY: LOW (SECURITY_REVIEW.md B-15) | OWASP A05 — Security Misconfiguration

PROBLEM: No Content-Security-Policy, X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Strict-Transport-Security, or Referrer-Policy headers are set. The embedded web UI is served without these browser-level protections, making it vulnerable to clickjacking, MIME-type sniffing, and missing HSTS preloading.

FIX: Add headers to the existing corsMiddleware() at internal/app/cors.go (or create a separate securityHeadersMiddleware if separation of concerns is preferred):

  c.Header('X-Content-Type-Options', 'nosniff')
  c.Header('X-Frame-Options', 'DENY')
  c.Header('Strict-Transport-Security', 'max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains')
  c.Header('Content-Security-Policy', "default-src 'self'")
  c.Header('Referrer-Policy', 'strict-origin-when-cross-origin')

CSP CONSIDERATION: The 'default-src self' policy is restrictive. The web UI may need 'script-src self unsafe-inline' if it uses inline scripts, or 'style-src self unsafe-inline' for inline styles. Test the web UI after applying CSP and adjust as needed. Start strict, relax only where necessary.

HSTS NOTE: Only set Strict-Transport-Security if the deployment uses TLS. Consider making this conditional on a config flag or detecting the X-Forwarded-Proto header.

FILES:
- internal/app/cors.go (add headers to existing middleware or create new middleware)

SCOPE: S (~2 hours) | BREAKING: No (may require CSP tuning for web UI)

comments.author: iznogoud
comments.text:
CSP TUNING GUIDE: The initial Content-Security-Policy should be strict (default-src 'self'). However, the embedded React web UI may break. Common issues:

1. Inline styles from component libraries → add 'unsafe-inline' to style-src (or use nonces)
2. Inline scripts from build tools → add 'unsafe-inline' to script-src (less secure) or configure Vite to externalize scripts
3. External fonts (Google Fonts, etc.) → add font source to font-src
4. API calls to different origin → add API origin to connect-src (if not same-origin)

TESTING APPROACH: Deploy with Content-Security-Policy-Report-Only header first. This logs violations without blocking them. Monitor browser console for CSP violations, adjust the policy, then switch to enforcing mode.

Report-Only header: Content-Security-Policy-Report-Only: default-src 'self'; report-uri /csp-report

title: F-03: Fetch admin permissions from backend instead of localStorage
description:
SEVERITY: HIGH (SECURITY_REVIEW.md F-03) | OWASP A01 — Broken Access Control

PROBLEM: Admin permission flags (including is_super_admin) are read directly from localStorage at web/src/utils/adminPermissions.ts:8-27. Any authenticated admin can open DevTools → Application → localStorage, set adminInfo.is_super_admin = true, refresh the page, and the UI renders all super-admin features and makes super-admin API calls.

CURRENT CODE (adminPermissions.ts:8-27):
  const stored = localStorage.getItem(USER_KEY_ADMIN)
  const info = JSON.parse(stored)
  // Permissions derived entirely from this client-stored object

While the backend will reject unauthorized API calls (the admin JWT doesn't grant super-admin), the UI rendering creates confusion, enables social engineering, and may expose admin-only UI elements that leak information about system capabilities.

FIX:
1. Modify useAdminPermissions hook (lines 34-54) to fetch permissions from GET /v0/admin/me on session initialization
2. Store fetched permissions in React context (not localStorage)
3. The GET /v0/admin/me endpoint returns the admin record with permissions — verify it exists and returns the needed fields
4. Cache the response for the session lifetime; re-fetch on page refresh

FILES:
- web/src/utils/adminPermissions.ts (fetch from backend, not localStorage)
- Verify GET /v0/admin/me endpoint exists in internal/http/api/admin/ handlers

SCOPE: S (~3 hours) | BREAKING: No

title: F-05: Prevent username enumeration via login prepare endpoint
description:
SEVERITY: HIGH (SECURITY_REVIEW.md F-05) | OWASP A07 — Identification and Authentication Failures

PROBLEM: The /login/prepare endpoint (called from web/src/pages/Login.tsx:116-136) returns different responses depending on whether the username exists: if the user exists, it returns MFA status (mfa_enabled, totp_enabled, passkey_enabled); if the user doesn't exist, it returns an error. This allows attackers to enumerate valid usernames by observing response differences, which is a prerequisite for B-01 (password reset) and B-02 (TOTP brute-force) attacks.

FIX — BACKEND:
1. Modify the LoginPrepare handler to return a CONSISTENT response shape regardless of whether the username exists:
   - If user exists: return actual MFA status
   - If user doesn't exist: return {mfa_enabled: false, totp_enabled: false, passkey_enabled: false} (same shape, no error)
2. Add a small random delay (50-200ms) using crypto/rand to prevent timing-based enumeration
3. Use constant-time comparison where possible

CONSIDERATION: This changes the UX slightly — the frontend can no longer show 'user not found' before attempting login. Instead, all users see the password prompt, and invalid usernames fail at the password step with a generic 'invalid credentials' message. This is standard security practice (see OWASP guidelines on authentication error messages).

FILES:
- internal/http/api/front/handlers/auth.go (LoginPrepare handler)
- internal/http/api/admin/handlers/auth.go (admin LoginPrepare handler, same fix)

SCOPE: S (~2 hours) | BREAKING: No (response shape preserved, just content changes)

comments.author: iznogoud
codex
comments.text: Claimed by codex. MCP Agent Mail is not available in this session, so coordination is being recorded here. Working on the backend login-prepare handlers in internal/http/api/front/handlers/mfa.go and internal/http/api/admin/handlers/mfa.go to normalize responses and add jitter.

comments.author: codex
comments.text: Completed. LoginPrepare now returns a consistent 200 response with false MFA flags for missing or disabled/inactive accounts, and both admin/front handlers add crypto-random jitter to reduce timing-based enumeration. Added regression tests in both handler packages.

title: B-16: Require current password for admin password changes (unless super-admin)
description:
SEVERITY: LOW (SECURITY_REVIEW.md B-16) | OWASP A01 — Broken Access Control

PROBLEM: PUT /v0/admin/admins/:id/password at internal/http/api/admin/handlers/admins.go:287-342 accepts a password field that directly sets a new password WITHOUT requiring the current password. Any admin with permission to this endpoint can take over other admin accounts by resetting their passwords.

FIX:
1. Add old_password field to the request body
2. If the caller is modifying their OWN password (adminID from JWT == :id param): require and validate old_password
3. If the caller is a super-admin modifying ANOTHER admin's password: allow without old_password (legitimate admin override)
4. Audit-log all admin password change events (who changed whose password, timestamp)

CONSIDERATION: This is a privilege management feature, not a critical vulnerability — the attacker must already be an authenticated admin with specific permissions. However, it violates the principle of least surprise (admins expect password changes to require the old password).

FILES:
- internal/http/api/admin/handlers/admins.go:287-342

SCOPE: S (~3 hours) | BREAKING: Yes (clients must now send old_password)

title: B-04: Enforce API key expiration in authentication query
description:
SEVERITY: HIGH (SECURITY_REVIEW.md B-04) | OWASP A01 — Broken Access Control

PROBLEM: The DBAPIKeyProvider.Authenticate method at internal/access/db_api_key_provider.go:82-85 queries for API keys using 'WHERE api_key = ? AND active = ? AND revoked_at IS NULL' but does NOT filter on expires_at. This means API keys with a set expiration date continue to function indefinitely after expiry. The models.APIKey struct has an ExpiresAt *time.Time field, and the model's Status() method already checks expiration for display purposes — but the actual authentication query ignores it entirely.

FIX: Add 'AND (expires_at IS NULL OR expires_at > ?)' with time.Now().UTC() to the WHERE clause. This is the safest fix in the entire plan — completely non-breaking (keys without expiry continue working; only actually-expired keys are affected, which is correct behavior).

FILES:
- internal/access/db_api_key_provider.go:82-85 (add expires_at condition)
- internal/access/db_api_key_provider_test.go (add 3 test cases)

SCOPE: S (~2 hours) | BREAKING: No | RISK: Minimal

comments.author: iznogoud
comments.text:
IMPLEMENTATION GUIDE: The fix is a single WHERE clause addition. Current query at db_api_key_provider.go:82-85:
  Where("api_key = ? AND active = ? AND revoked_at IS NULL", token, true)
Change to:
  Where("api_key = ? AND active = ? AND revoked_at IS NULL AND (expires_at IS NULL OR expires_at > ?)", token, true, time.Now().UTC())

The 'expires_at IS NULL' clause is critical — most API keys don't have an expiration set (ExpiresAt is *time.Time, nil by default). Without it, ALL keys would stop working.

VERIFICATION: After fix, create a test API key with expires_at in the past via direct DB insert, attempt to authenticate with it — must get 401. Create one with future expires_at — must succeed. Create one with NULL expires_at — must succeed.
